title: Post and rail fencing for stock
subtitle: How to set posts and fix rails to build a fence that holds cattle, horses, and large livestock.
excerpt: Post and rail fencing is the standard boundary fence for paddocks and fields holding large livestock. It is not a quick fence to build, but it is a long-lasting one; correctly set posts and sound rails will last 20 to 30 years.
sourceNotes: The British Horse Society, field safety and fencing guidance. AHDB, farm infrastructure guide. Forest Research, wood preservative and post longevity data.

glossaryTerms.definition: A heavy steel tube with closed ends and handles, dropped over a fence post and driven into the ground by the weight and force of lifting and dropping. Faster than using a sledgehammer for softwood posts in most soils. Not effective in very stony ground where a post-hole digger must be used instead.

The heavy end post at corners, gates, and changes of direction in a fence line. Wider and set deeper than intermediate posts, because it absorbs the full tension of the wire or rails. A fence that fails at a corner or gate usually has an undersized or poorly set strainer post.

content.text: Post and rail is appropriate for paddocks holding horses, cattle, and large livestock. It gives animals a visible boundary, is unlikely to cause injury, and does not require the tensioning and maintenance that wire fencing needs. The two critical elements are post depth and rail fixing method.
Pressure-treated softwood posts (typically 75 mm x 75 mm or 100 mm x 100 mm section) are standard. Intermediate posts are set at 1.8 to 2.4 metre spacing and driven or set 600 mm into the ground.
at corners and gates should be 150 mm x 150 mm minimum and set at least 900 mm deep, ideally in concrete for a permanent installation.

for intermediate posts in medium to soft ground. Check the post is vertical in two directions before driving deeply; a crooked post is very difficult to correct once it is 400 mm in. Use a spirit level against the post face. Drive until the post no longer moves on impact, which indicates adequate soil resistance.

Two or three rails per fence panel is standard for most livestock; three for horses, which push and lean. Rails are fixed to the inner face of the post (the paddock side) for livestock; they push against the rail, which pushes against the post into the ground, rather than pushing the rail off the nail. Nail through the rail into the post with two 100 mm galvanised nails per fixing, staggered to prevent splitting.

Walk fence lines in spring and autumn. Look for: posts that have lifted or tilted (frost heave or softening of the post base in wet soil), rails that have cracked or are pulling away from the posts, and any loose nails. A rail that is cracked lengthwise along the grain can be left if it is still structurally sound; a rail cracked across the grain should be replaced. Re-nail any loose fixings before they are pushed off by livestock pressure.
